title: Essential Thrombocythaemia
oneLiner: Myeloproliferative neoplasm with persistently high platelets; risk of thrombosis and haemorrhage; often incidental finding but can kill.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute arterial thrombosis** (stroke / MI / limb ischaemia)  
  *Single finding*: sudden focal neurology, chest pain with dynamic ECG changes, or pulseless cold limb — treat per acute stroke/MI protocols immediately, do not wait for haematology.
- **Acute venous thromboembolism** (PE / mesenteric vein thrombosis)  
  *Single finding*: unexplained severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam + lactate rise → suspect mesenteric ischaemia; or sudden dyspnoea, hypoxia, right heart strain — CT angiogram needed.
- **Major haemorrhage due to acquired von Willebrand syndrome (aVWS)**  
  *Single finding*: platelet count >1,500 × 10⁹/L **plus** spontaneous bruising/bleeding (epistaxis, GI, intracranial) → check ristocetin cofactor activity urgently; avoid antiplatelet therapy until cleared.
sections.Presentation:
- **Asymptomatic** (50-60%): discovered on routine FBC – platelet count >450 × 10⁹/L persistent
- **Microvascular symptoms**: headache, dizziness, visual disturbances (blurring, scotomata), erythromelalgia (burning pain + erythema of hands/feet), transient ischaemic attacks
- **Thrombotic events**: DVT, PE, stroke, TIA, MI, splanchnic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis)
- **Bleeding events**: epistaxis, easy bruising, GI bleeding, haematuria
- **Constitutional symptoms**: fatigue, pruritus (especially after warm bath), night sweats, weight loss (less common than in polycythaemia vera)
- **Exam findings**: splenomegaly (~30%), signs of thrombosis (swollen leg, focal neurology, chest signs), signs of bleeding (ecchymoses, conjunctival petechiae)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
| Condition | One-line differentiator |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Reactive (secondary) thrombocytosis** | CRP ↑, ferritin ↑, iron deficiency (microcytic hypochromic), no JAK2/CALR/MPL mutation |
| **Polycythaemia vera (PV)** | Haematocrit ↑ >0.48 (women) / >0.52 (men), JAK2 V617F positive, often with pruritus and gout |
| **Primary myelofibrosis** | Splenomegaly >10cm below costal margin, leucoerythroblastic blood film, bone marrow fibrosis |
| **Chronic myeloid leukaemia** | Leucocytosis >25 × 10⁹/L, basophilia, BCR-ABL translocation, splenomegaly |
| **Familial thrombocytosis** | Young age, multiple family members, no JAK2/CALR/MPL mutation (consider THPO or MPL germline mutations) |

**Splitter:** JAK2 V617F mutation present → MPN; absent → consider reactive thrombocytosis or other MPN (CALR/MPL mutations needed). Also: elevated CRP + normal ferritin + no mutation → likely reactive.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG (if chest pain), NEWS2, consider focused ultrasound for DVT if symptomatic, neurological examination
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC with film (clumps? giant platelets? blast cells?)  
  - CRP, ferritin, iron studies, LFTs, U&E, clotting screen  
  - JAK2 V617F mutation (blood) – result may take 2-5 days; if negative, request **CALR** and **MPL** mutations  
  - If bleeding suspicion: **ristocetin cofactor activity**, factor VIII, vWF antigen  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: none routinely unless thrombosis suspected – CT head (stroke), CTPA (PE), CT abdomen with contrast (mesenteric ischaemia/splanchnic vein thrombosis)  
  - Second-line: MRI/MRA if neurological symptoms without clear infarct  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (for diagnosis, not emergency) – shows megakaryocytic hyperplasia, no fibrosis in ET; reserved for atypical presentations or when cytoreduction planned  
- **Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2016):**  
  - Platelet count ≥450 × 10⁹/L  
  - Bone marrow biopsy showing megakaryocytic proliferation without marked fibrosis  
  - Not meeting criteria for other MPNs (PV, PMF, CML, MDS)  
  - Presence of JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutation (or clonal marker)  
  - *If no mutation:* exclude reactive causes for ≥3 months  
- **Never-miss test:** **JAK2 V617F mutation** – omission leads to failure to diagnose MPN and inappropriate reassurance or treatment
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If acute thrombosis: treat as per standard (thrombolysis/anticoagulation) – contact haematology early to discuss interaction with platelet count  
  - If major haemorrhage + platelet count >1,500: withhold aspirin, give DDAVP (0.3 mcg/kg IV) or vWF concentrate if aVWS confirmed, platelet transfusion may be ineffective; contact haematology  
  - If erythromelalgia: aspirin 75mg (if no bleeding risk) – symptoms often improve dramatically within hours  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Risk stratify:  
    - **High risk:** age >60 **OR** prior thrombosis (arterial or venous) **OR** platelet count >1,500 with bleeding  
    - **Low risk:** all other patients  
  - High risk: start cytoreduction – hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) 500-1500mg daily [check local policy for dose titration], or peginterferon alfa-2a in younger patients (<60) and pregnant  
  - Low risk: aspirin 75mg daily (if no contraindication), control cardiovascular risk factors  
  - If splenic or hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari): immediate anticoagulation (LMWH then warfarin) and contact haematology for possible TIPS or transplant assessment  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Monitor FBC every 1-3 months; adjust cytoreduction to keep platelets <400-600 × 10⁹/L (no rigid target, but <400 if high risk)  
  - Manage iron deficiency if present (do not replace unless needed – can worsen thrombocytosis in ET)  
  - Annual review: splenomegaly, symptom assessment (pruritus, fatigue), consider bone marrow every 2-5 years if transformation suspected  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl if NBM, otherwise oral hydration  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg OD) **only if platelet count <1,500 and no bleeding** – if >1,500, avoid until cytoreduction and aVWS excluded  
  - Diet: normal unless post-procedure  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable; 1-hourly if acute thrombosis/haemorrhage
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**  
  - Aspirin 75mg PO once daily (long-term in low risk)  
  - Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) 500mg-1.5g PO daily – start 500mg OD, titrate weekly by 500mg to target platelet count; [check local policy for exact titration]  
  - Peginterferon alfa-2a 45-180mcg SC weekly – [check local protocol] (preferred in pregnancy, younger patients)  
- **Second-line:**  
  - Anagrelide 0.5mg PO BD, increase by 0.5mg weekly up to 2mg total daily; [check local policy] – used if hydroxycarbamide intolerant/ineffective; risk of cardiomyopathy  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Aspirin + anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs) → bleeding risk (avoid unless definite indication)  
  - Hydroxycarbamide + live vaccines (avoid)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Hydroxycarbamide: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (consult local BNF/specialist)  
  - Anagrelide: avoid in severe hepatic impairment; dose reduce in moderate  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Aspirin in aVWS** (platelet >1,500 with low von Willebrand factor) → catastrophic haemorrhage. Always check ristocetin cofactor before starting aspirin in patients with platelet count >1,500.  
  - **Hydroxycarbamide overdose** (e.g., >2g/day) → severe myelosuppression (pancytopenia). Monitor FBC every 2 weeks until stable.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Thrombosis (stroke, MI, PE, portal/splanchnic vein thrombosis)  
  - Major haemorrhage (especially if concurrent aspirin + high platelets + aVWS)  
  - Erythromelalgia (if untreated)  
  - Thrombotic microangiopathy (rare)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Progressive splenomegaly  
  - Transformation to **myelofibrosis** (~5-10% after 10-15 years) or **acute myeloid leukaemia** (~1-3% after 10 years, higher risk with alkylating agents)  
  - Iron deficiency due to chronic blood loss (GI angiodysplasia)  
  - Pruritus, fatigue, constitutional symptoms
sections.Prognosis:
- Median survival near-normal for low risk (no prior thrombosis, age <60); high risk (age >60 or prior thrombosis) has increased mortality due to thrombotic events.  
- Transformation to myelofibrosis or AML reduces survival to 2-5 years from diagnosis of transformation.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check JAK2 V617F** in unexplained thrombocytosis – this is the most efficient way to confirm ET vs reactive. If negative, order CALR/MPL.  
- **Do not give aspirin until platelet count <1,500 or aVWS excluded** – a single bleeding event can be fatal.  
- **If young (<40) with isolated thrombocytosis and no splenomegaly**, consider hereditary thrombocytosis and test family members; also consider iron deficiency as cause.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Telling patient "just high platelets" without investigating** – missing ET leads to avoidable strokes.  
- **Prescribing aspirin for low risk ET without checking for contraindications** – not harmful if platelets normal, but in aVWS can cause catastrophic bleed.  
- **Using anagrelide as first-line in young patients** – avoid; peginterferon is preferred due to lower risk of leukaemic transformation.  
- **Starting cytoreduction for asymptomatic low risk patients** – unnecessary; aspirin + surveillance is appropriate.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New focal weakness, speech change, transient vision loss, chest pain, dyspnoea (thrombosis)  
  - Unexplained bruising, epistaxis lasting >15 minutes, black/melaena stools, haematemesis (bleeding)  
  - Severe burning pain in hands/feet (erythromelalgia)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Haematology clinic within 4 weeks (or sooner if abnormal JAK2 result is pending)  
  - Repeat FBC in 1-2 weeks if starting aspirin or cytoreduction
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/haematology registrar urgently if:**  
  - Acute arterial or venous thrombotic event (stroke, MI, PE, mesenteric ischaemia) – need coordinated care  
  - Major haemorrhage (especially GI, intracranial) with platelet count >1,500  
  - Platelet count >1,500 + symptoms of aVWS (bleeding)  
  - Splenic or hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (indicating deterioration), lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS drop >1 point, new haemodynamic instability

rawMarkdown:
# Essential Thrombocythaemia

**One-liner:** Myeloproliferative neoplasm with persistently high platelets; risk of thrombosis and haemorrhage; often incidental finding but can kill.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute arterial thrombosis** (stroke / MI / limb ischaemia)  
  *Single finding*: sudden focal neurology, chest pain with dynamic ECG changes, or pulseless cold limb — treat per acute stroke/MI protocols immediately, do not wait for haematology.
- **Acute venous thromboembolism** (PE / mesenteric vein thrombosis)  
  *Single finding*: unexplained severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam + lactate rise → suspect mesenteric ischaemia; or sudden dyspnoea, hypoxia, right heart strain — CT angiogram needed.
- **Major haemorrhage due to acquired von Willebrand syndrome (aVWS)**  
  *Single finding*: platelet count >1,500 × 10⁹/L **plus** spontaneous bruising/bleeding (epistaxis, GI, intracranial) → check ristocetin cofactor activity urgently; avoid antiplatelet therapy until cleared.

### Presentation
- **Asymptomatic** (50-60%): discovered on routine FBC – platelet count >450 × 10⁹/L persistent
- **Microvascular symptoms**: headache, dizziness, visual disturbances (blurring, scotomata), erythromelalgia (burning pain + erythema of hands/feet), transient ischaemic attacks
- **Thrombotic events**: DVT, PE, stroke, TIA, MI, splanchnic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis)
- **Bleeding events**: epistaxis, easy bruising, GI bleeding, haematuria
- **Constitutional symptoms**: fatigue, pruritus (especially after warm bath), night sweats, weight loss (less common than in polycythaemia vera)
- **Exam findings**: splenomegaly (~30%), signs of thrombosis (swollen leg, focal neurology, chest signs), signs of bleeding (ecchymoses, conjunctival petechiae)

### Differential Diagnosis
| Condition | One-line differentiator |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Reactive (secondary) thrombocytosis** | CRP ↑, ferritin ↑, iron deficiency (microcytic hypochromic), no JAK2/CALR/MPL mutation |
| **Polycythaemia vera (PV)** | Haematocrit ↑ >0.48 (women) / >0.52 (men), JAK2 V617F positive, often with pruritus and gout |
| **Primary myelofibrosis** | Splenomegaly >10cm below costal margin, leucoerythroblastic blood film, bone marrow fibrosis |
| **Chronic myeloid leukaemia** | Leucocytosis >25 × 10⁹/L, basophilia, BCR-ABL translocation, splenomegaly |
| **Familial thrombocytosis** | Young age, multiple family members, no JAK2/CALR/MPL mutation (consider THPO or MPL germline mutations) |

**Splitter:** JAK2 V617F mutation present → MPN; absent → consider reactive thrombocytosis or other MPN (CALR/MPL mutations needed). Also: elevated CRP + normal ferritin + no mutation → likely reactive.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG (if chest pain), NEWS2, consider focused ultrasound for DVT if symptomatic, neurological examination
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC with film (clumps? giant platelets? blast cells?)  
  - CRP, ferritin, iron studies, LFTs, U&E, clotting screen  
  - JAK2 V617F mutation (blood) – result may take 2-5 days; if negative, request **CALR** and **MPL** mutations  
  - If bleeding suspicion: **ristocetin cofactor activity**, factor VIII, vWF antigen  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: none routinely unless thrombosis suspected – CT head (stroke), CTPA (PE), CT abdomen with contrast (mesenteric ischaemia/splanchnic vein thrombosis)  
  - Second-line: MRI/MRA if neurological symptoms without clear infarct  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Bone marrow aspirate + trephine (for diagnosis, not emergency) – shows megakaryocytic hyperplasia, no fibrosis in ET; reserved for atypical presentations or when cytoreduction planned  
- **Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2016):**  
  - Platelet count ≥450 × 10⁹/L  
  - Bone marrow biopsy showing megakaryocytic proliferation without marked fibrosis  
  - Not meeting criteria for other MPNs (PV, PMF, CML, MDS)  
  - Presence of JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutation (or clonal marker)  
  - *If no mutation:* exclude reactive causes for ≥3 months  
- **Never-miss test:** **JAK2 V617F mutation** – omission leads to failure to diagnose MPN and inappropriate reassurance or treatment

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If acute thrombosis: treat as per standard (thrombolysis/anticoagulation) – contact haematology early to discuss interaction with platelet count  
  - If major haemorrhage + platelet count >1,500: withhold aspirin, give DDAVP (0.3 mcg/kg IV) or vWF concentrate if aVWS confirmed, platelet transfusion may be ineffective; contact haematology  
  - If erythromelalgia: aspirin 75mg (if no bleeding risk) – symptoms often improve dramatically within hours  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Risk stratify:  
    - **High risk:** age >60 **OR** prior thrombosis (arterial or venous) **OR** platelet count >1,500 with bleeding  
    - **Low risk:** all other patients  
  - High risk: start cytoreduction – hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) 500-1500mg daily [check local policy for dose titration], or peginterferon alfa-2a in younger patients (<60) and pregnant  
  - Low risk: aspirin 75mg daily (if no contraindication), control cardiovascular risk factors  
  - If splenic or hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari): immediate anticoagulation (LMWH then warfarin) and contact haematology for possible TIPS or transplant assessment  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Monitor FBC every 1-3 months; adjust cytoreduction to keep platelets <400-600 × 10⁹/L (no rigid target, but <400 if high risk)  
  - Manage iron deficiency if present (do not replace unless needed – can worsen thrombocytosis in ET)  
  - Annual review: splenomegaly, symptom assessment (pruritus, fatigue), consider bone marrow every 2-5 years if transformation suspected  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% NaCl if NBM, otherwise oral hydration  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg OD) **only if platelet count <1,500 and no bleeding** – if >1,500, avoid until cytoreduction and aVWS excluded  
  - Diet: normal unless post-procedure  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable; 1-hourly if acute thrombosis/haemorrhage

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**  
  - Aspirin 75mg PO once daily (long-term in low risk)  
  - Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) 500mg-1.5g PO daily – start 500mg OD, titrate weekly by 500mg to target platelet count; [check local policy for exact titration]  
  - Peginterferon alfa-2a 45-180mcg SC weekly – [check local protocol] (preferred in pregnancy, younger patients)  
- **Second-line:**  
  - Anagrelide 0.5mg PO BD, increase by 0.5mg weekly up to 2mg total daily; [check local policy] – used if hydroxycarbamide intolerant/ineffective; risk of cardiomyopathy  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Aspirin + anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs) → bleeding risk (avoid unless definite indication)  
  - Hydroxycarbamide + live vaccines (avoid)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Hydroxycarbamide: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (consult local BNF/specialist)  
  - Anagrelide: avoid in severe hepatic impairment; dose reduce in moderate  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Aspirin in aVWS** (platelet >1,500 with low von Willebrand factor) → catastrophic haemorrhage. Always check ristocetin cofactor before starting aspirin in patients with platelet count >1,500.  
  - **Hydroxycarbamide overdose** (e.g., >2g/day) → severe myelosuppression (pancytopenia). Monitor FBC every 2 weeks until stable.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Thrombosis (stroke, MI, PE, portal/splanchnic vein thrombosis)  
  - Major haemorrhage (especially if concurrent aspirin + high platelets + aVWS)  
  - Erythromelalgia (if untreated)  
  - Thrombotic microangiopathy (rare)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Progressive splenomegaly  
  - Transformation to **myelofibrosis** (~5-10% after 10-15 years) or **acute myeloid leukaemia** (~1-3% after 10 years, higher risk with alkylating agents)  
  - Iron deficiency due to chronic blood loss (GI angiodysplasia)  
  - Pruritus, fatigue, constitutional symptoms

### Prognosis
- Median survival near-normal for low risk (no prior thrombosis, age <60); high risk (age >60 or prior thrombosis) has increased mortality due to thrombotic events.  
- Transformation to myelofibrosis or AML reduces survival to 2-5 years from diagnosis of transformation.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check JAK2 V617F** in unexplained thrombocytosis – this is the most efficient way to confirm ET vs reactive. If negative, order CALR/MPL.  
- **Do not give aspirin until platelet count <1,500 or aVWS excluded** – a single bleeding event can be fatal.  
- **If young (<40) with isolated thrombocytosis and no splenomegaly**, consider hereditary thrombocytosis and test family members; also consider iron deficiency as cause.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Telling patient "just high platelets" without investigating** – missing ET leads to avoidable strokes.  
- **Prescribing aspirin for low risk ET without checking for contraindications** – not harmful if platelets normal, but in aVWS can cause catastrophic bleed.  
- **Using anagrelide as first-line in young patients** – avoid; peginterferon is preferred due to lower risk of leukaemic transformation.  
- **Starting cytoreduction for asymptomatic low risk patients** – unnecessary; aspirin + surveillance is appropriate.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New focal weakness, speech change, transient vision loss, chest pain, dyspnoea (thrombosis)  
  - Unexplained bruising, epistaxis lasting >15 minutes, black/melaena stools, haematemesis (bleeding)  
  - Severe burning pain in hands/feet (erythromelalgia)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Haematology clinic within 4 weeks (or sooner if abnormal JAK2 result is pending)  
  - Repeat FBC in 1-2 weeks if starting aspirin or cytoreduction

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/haematology registrar urgently if:**  
  - Acute arterial or venous thrombotic event (stroke, MI, PE, mesenteric ischaemia) – need coordinated care  
  - Major haemorrhage (especially GI, intracranial) with platelet count >1,500  
  - Platelet count >1,500 + symptoms of aVWS (bleeding)  
  - Splenic or hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (indicating deterioration), lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS drop >1 point, new haemodynamic instability
